title: IQI Placement, Dark Adaptation, and Film Identification
narrationScript: A radiograph is evidence, and evidence has to prove its own chain of custody. Before you are allowed to say one word about the weld, the film has to demonstrate that it was shot right, that it can show what it claims to show, and that anyone who picks it up for the next forty years can tell exactly what it is. That proof is built from the image quality indicator, the density, the identification, and the location markers. In this lesson we verify the first group. I Q I placement, the condition of your own eyes, and film identification. Start with the I Q I, because it is the most abused element on production film. The principle is simple. The indicator must experience the same radiographic conditions as the area of interest. Same beam quality, same thickness, same geometry, same film, same processing. Bend any one of those and the sensitivity it demonstrates is fiction. Side comes first. The source side of the part is the standard position. An indicator on the source side sits as far from the film as a flaw can sit, so it is imaged with all the geometric unsharpness the technique can inflict. Put it on the film side instead and it lies closer to the film than most of the volume you are testing. It images sharper than the real flaws, and it flatters the technique. Film side placement is allowed only when the source side cannot be reached, as in many double wall shots and closed systems, and it must be flagged with a lead letter F placed next to the indicator. When you see the F, you know exactly what was demonstrated. When the geometry says the source side was unreachable and there is no F on the film, the radiograph has a problem. Position is next. A hole type plaque goes on the base metal beside the weld, parallel to it, not on the crown, and its identifying numbers stay clear of the area of interest. The shim handles the weld reinforcement. A shim of radiographically similar material goes under the plaque so the total thickness under the indicator matches the weld thickness with its reinforcement included. The shim must be bigger than the plaque, and the usual expectation is that the plaque outline shows clear of the shim edge on at least three sides in the image, which proves the plaque really sat on shim material. A wire indicator works the other way around. It goes directly on the weld, across it, with the wires perpendicular to the weld length, so the wires cross the area of interest you are grading. Number matters too. One indicator per radiograph is the usual baseline. A panoramic shot that exposes a full circumference in one exposure typically carries at least three indicators spaced about a hundred twenty degrees apart, because sensitivity proven at one corner of a huge exposure proves nothing about the far side. Now your eyes. Reading film is low light, low contrast work, and the retina needs time to get there. Spend several minutes adapting in the subdued viewing room before you evaluate, and allow longer if you walked in from bright sunlight. Many procedures write five or ten minutes into the requirement. Use the variable intensity on the illuminator so you are not dazzled between a thin film and a dense one. And there is a formal side. Your employer written practice, built on industry recommended practices, requires a documented near vision check at least annually. A typical requirement is reading Jaeger number one size text at a distance of not less than twelve inches, with at least one eye, natural or corrected. Corrected is fine. Showing up to read film without the glasses you were tested with is not. There is also a requirement to demonstrate that you can distinguish colors and close shades of gray, because a person who cannot separate gray tones cannot grade film. Last, identification. Every radiograph must carry permanent, unique identification that ties it to the job and to the exact weld. Typical content is the company or contractor, the job or contract number, the part and weld or seam number, the view or station, and the date. Repair films get flagged as well, R one for the first repair examination, R two for the second. The marks are made with lead characters that expose onto the film with the image, or with an identification block printed onto a film corner with light in the darkroom. Either way, the identification must never lie over the area of interest, and it must match the technique record exactly. A beautiful film that cannot be traced to its weld is scrap. So is a beautiful weld behind a film whose quality cannot be proven. Verify the indicator, verify your eyes, verify the identification, and only then start reading.
imagePrompt: A radiographer kneels beside a chrome-moly pipe weld, setting a small lead image-quality indicator on a thin metal shim next to the weld bead while a film cassette strapped beneath the joint waits for the exposure.

sections.heading: The Radiograph Must Prove Itself
sections.body:
Interpretation does not begin with the weld - it begins with the film proving its own quality. Four pieces of evidence carry that proof: the **image quality indicator** and its placement, the **density** in the area of interest, the **identification** that ties the film to one specific weld, and the **location markers** that demonstrate coverage. The interpreter is the gatekeeper: if any of those four cannot be verified at the viewing bench, the radiograph is not interpretable, and the answer is a reshoot - no matter how clean the weld image looks.

This lesson covers the first half of the gate. You will verify that the IQI sits where the rules put it, confirm that your own eyes are qualified and adapted for the work, and check that the film's identification makes it permanent, traceable evidence. The next lesson finishes the gate with location markers, density measurement, and the artifacts that try to fool you. None of this is clerical. A misplaced IQI quietly overstates sensitivity, and an untraceable film is worthless the day a weld leaks and the record is pulled.

sections.heading: IQI Placement: Side, Position, and Number
sections.body:
The governing principle: the IQI must experience the **same radiographic conditions as the area of interest** - same beam quality, thickness, geometry, film, and processing. Every placement rule serves that principle.

- **Side.** The **source side** is the required default. A source-side IQI sits as far from the film as an internal flaw can sit, so it suffers the technique's full geometric unsharpness. A film-side IQI sits closer to the film than the volume under test, images sharper than the real flaws, and flatters the technique - which is why film-side placement is permitted only when the source side is inaccessible, and must be flagged with a **lead letter F** adjacent to the IQI.
- **Position.** A **hole-type plaque** lies on base metal **beside and parallel to the weld**, on a shim, with its identification numbers clear of the area of interest. A **wire IQI** lies **on the weld**, with the wires **perpendicular to the weld axis** so they cross the region being graded.
- **Number.** At least one IQI per radiograph is the baseline. A **panoramic** exposure covering a full circumference in one shot typically carries **at least three IQIs spaced about 120 degrees apart**, and codes add IQIs where film density varies beyond allowed limits across an exposure.

sections.caption: IQI placement rules in section and plan view: source side preferred, hole-type plaque shimmed beside the weld, wire IQI across the weld, lead F flagging film-side placement.

sections.heading: Shims and the Film-Side F
sections.body:
A weld with reinforcement is thicker than the base metal beside it. A plaque IQI laid on bare base metal would be judged against the **thinner** section - an easier test than the weld actually presents. The **shim** closes that gap: a piece of **radiographically similar material**, placed under the plaque, thick enough that the total thickness under the IQI matches the weld thickness including reinforcement on both sides. Sensitivity demonstrated through plaque-plus-shim then honestly represents the weld.

The shim must also be **larger than the plaque** - the common expectation is that the plaque's outline is visible clear of the shim edge on at least three sides of its image, proving on the film itself that the IQI sat fully on shim material rather than hanging off an edge.

The **lead F** is the other honesty device. It marks film-side placement so no one later assumes a source-side demonstration. At the bench, work the logic both ways: an F on a film whose geometry allowed source-side placement is a procedure violation to question, and a film-side setup with **no F** is an unidentified, nonconforming demonstration. The F must sit adjacent to the IQI without masking it or the area of interest.

sections.title: What 2-2T on the technique sheet commits you to
sections.body: A **2-2T** quality level means the plaque thickness is **2% of the part thickness** and the **2T hole** - diameter twice the plaque thickness - must be visible. The interpreter confirms the **essential hole or wire is actually visible across the entire area of interest** at the required density, on every radiograph. Codes publish equivalence tables between hole-type and wire IQIs; what never changes is that the demonstration must be visible to *you*, at *this* viewing station, before any weld call is made.

sections.heading: Dark Adaptation and Visual Acuity
sections.body:
Film reading is low-light, low-contrast work, and the interpreter's eyes are instruments with their own calibration requirements.

**Adaptation.** The retina needs time to reach the sensitivity that dense film demands. Spend **several minutes in the subdued viewing room before evaluating** - many procedures specify five to ten minutes - and allow longer after bright sunlight. Protect the adaptation you build: use the illuminator's variable intensity between films of different density, keep masking tight, and never stare into an unmasked port.

**Acuity.** Employer written practices based on documents such as SNT-TC-1A and NAS 410 require a documented **near-vision examination at least annually**: a typical requirement is reading **Jaeger J-1 letters at not less than 12 inches**, with at least one eye, natural or corrected. If you passed wearing correction, you read film wearing it. A **color and gray-shade differentiation** check is also required at defined intervals, because grading film is the art of separating close gray tones. These checks protect the product and the technician - report changes in your vision rather than waiting for the next exam to find them.

sections.heading: Film Identification and Traceability
sections.body:
Every radiograph must carry **permanent, unique identification** linking it to one specific examination. Typical required content:

- Company or contractor performing the examination
- Job, contract, or work-order number
- Part, weld, or seam identification and the **view or station**
- Date of the exposure
- Repair status - **R1** for the first repair radiograph of a weld, **R2** for the second, so the film history of a repaired joint reads in order

Two methods dominate: **lead characters** laid on or near the part so their images expose with the radiograph, and darkroom **light-flashed identification blocks** printed onto a film corner. Either is acceptable when permanent and legible; markings must **never obscure the area of interest**. Double-loaded films from one exposure carry the same identification.

At the bench, verify the film identification **matches the technique record and report** exactly - weld numbers, station, date, repair status. A mismatch makes the film worthless as evidence: years from now, that radiograph may be the only proof of what was in a weld the day it entered service.

sections.caption: A simulated production radiograph as it reaches the viewing bench - five placement and identification elements to verify, two of which violate typical code rules.

figures.title: Verify before you read: simulated production radiograph

interactive.title: Spot the Placement Errors
interactive.intro: This simulated production radiograph just arrived for interpretation. Two of its placement elements violate typical code rules. Click the elements you would reject.

regions.label: Hole-type IQI on the weld crown
regions.feedback: Error. A hole-type plaque belongs on base metal beside the weld, on a shim that compensates for reinforcement - never perched on the irregular weld crown where it cannot sit flat or represent the section honestly.

regions.label: Hole-type IQI on a shim beside the weld
regions.feedback: Correct practice. The plaque lies parallel to the weld on a radiographically similar shim, the shim outline shows beyond the plaque edges, and the ID numbers stay clear of the weld image.

regions.label: Wire IQI
regions.feedback: Error. The wire IQI is on the weld - good - but its wires run along the weld axis. Wires must lie perpendicular to the weld length so they cross the area of interest being graded.

regions.label: Location marker
regions.feedback: Correct practice. Lead location markers appear at the ends of the exposure, clear of the weld image, mapping the film back to positions marked on the part.

regions.label: Identification block
regions.feedback: Correct practice. Permanent identification - job, weld, view, date, repair status - sits in the film corner where it obscures nothing, and it must match the technique record exactly.

checkQuestions.question: A radiograph shows a lead letter F beside the IQI. What does it indicate?
options: The film failed its density check
The IQI was placed on the film side of the part because the source side was inaccessible
The exposure was made with a fast film class
The weld was examined after final heat treatment
checkQuestions.explanation: The lead F flags film-side IQI placement, which is permitted only when the source side cannot be reached; without the F, a film-side demonstration would masquerade as the more demanding source-side one.

checkQuestions.question: Why is a shim placed under a hole-type IQI when radiographing a weld with reinforcement?
options: To raise the IQI closer to the source for a sharper image
To protect the IQI from weld spatter
So the total thickness under the IQI matches the weld thickness including reinforcement, making the demonstrated sensitivity honest
To increase the film density under the IQI
checkQuestions.explanation: Without the shim the IQI would be judged against the thinner base metal - an easier test than the weld presents; radiographically similar shim material restores a representative total thickness.

checkQuestions.question: A panoramic exposure radiographs a complete vessel girth weld in one shot. How are IQIs typically arranged?
options: One IQI anywhere on the circumference
At least three IQIs spaced approximately 120 degrees apart
Two IQIs placed side by side at the nameplate
No IQI is required for panoramic exposures
checkQuestions.explanation: Sensitivity proven at one point of a full-circumference exposure says nothing about the far side, so codes typically require at least three IQIs at roughly 120-degree intervals.

checkQuestions.question: An interpreter walks straight from bright sunlight into the viewing room. Before evaluating low-contrast detail, the correct action is to:
options: Begin reading immediately while visual memory of daylight is fresh
Turn the illuminator to maximum to overpower the daylight adaptation
Spend several minutes - longer than usual - adapting in the subdued room before making any evaluation
Brighten the room lights to match the outdoor light
checkQuestions.explanation: The retina must adapt down to film-reading luminances; procedures commonly require several minutes of adaptation, and more after bright daylight, before low-contrast detail can genuinely be seen.

checkQuestions.question: Which set of markings satisfies typical film identification requirements?
options: The weld number written on the film envelope in marker
Permanent, unique markings on the film - company, job, weld and view, date, and repair status such as R1 - placed clear of the area of interest and matching the technique record
Any identification, provided it is centered on the weld image for visibility
A verbal record kept by the radiographer who shot the film
checkQuestions.explanation: Identification must be permanent on the film itself, traceable to the exact weld and examination, must not obscure the area of interest, and must agree with the technique documentation.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Radiographic Interpretation and Evaluation Course 1.4-1.6
Codes such as ASME BPVC Section V, Article 2 (IQI placement, shims, and identification, general reference)
SNT-TC-1A and NAS 410 (near-vision and color perception examination expectations, general reference)
